Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the node positions, I can now map out the 4 grids:
Top-left grid (rows 2-23, cols 4-25):
- Row 0: blue (2-7, 4-9), red (2-7, 12-17), blue (2-7, 20-25)
- Row 1: red (10-15, 4-9), center (10-15, 12-17), blue (10-15, 20-25)
- Row 2: blue (18-23, 4-9), red (18-23, 12-17), red (18-23, 20-25)
Top-right grid (rows 2-23, cols 38-59):
- Row 0: blue (2-7, 38-43), red (2-7, 46-51), blue (2-7, 54-59)
- Row 1: red (10-15, 38-43), center (10-15, 46-51), red (10-15, 54-59)
- Row 2: blue (18-23, 38-43), red (18-23, 46-51), blue (18-23, 54-59)
Bottom-left grid (rows 36-57, cols 4-25):
- Row 0: red (36-41, 4-9), blue (36-41, 12-17), blue (36-41, 20-25)
- Row 1: red (44-49, 4-9), center (44-49, 12-17), red (44-49, 20-25)
- Row 2: blue (52-57, 4-9), blue (52-57, 12-17), red (52-57, 20-25)
Bottom-right grid (rows 36-57, cols 36-57):
- Row 0: blue (36-41, 36-41), blue (36-41, 44-49), blue (36-41, 52-57)
- Row 1: blue (44-49, 36-41), center (44-49, 44-49), blue (44-49, 52-57)
- Row 2: blue (52-57, 36-41), blue (52-57, 44-49), blue (52-57, 52-57)
So the bottom-right grid is ALL BLUE except for the center cell! The other grids have a mix of red and blue.
Now, the bottom-right grid is framed with charcoal and gray corners. This suggests it's the "target" or "active" grid. The goal might be to make the bottom-right grid match one of the other grids by clicking on cells.
But I've clicked on many cells and nothing changes! What if the game requires clicking on cells in the bottom-right grid to change their color? And the color cycles through some palette?
Let me try clicking on a blue cell in the bottom-right grid. The top-left blue cell is at rows 36-41, cols 36-41. Let me click at row 38, col 38.
Wait, but I already tried clicking on blue cells (node 39 is blue at rows 36-41, cols 36-41). Let me check which nodes I've clicked on before...
Actually, looking at my history, I clicked on:
1. Row 2, col 2 (blue block in top-left grid)
2. Row 12, col 14 (red in center cell)
3. Row 10, col 14 (white in center cell)
4. Row 32, col 34 (gray corner of frame)
5. Row 32, col 50 (charcoal frame)
6. Row 63, col 32 (orange bar)
7. Row 46, col 46 (red in bottom-right center)
8. Row 2, col 2 (repeated)
9. Row 2, col 2 (repeated)
10. Row 8, col 14 (black in top-left center)
11. Row 6, col 6 (intersection)
12. Row 10, col 12 (gray in top-left center)
I haven't clicked on a blue cell in the bottom-right grid! Let me try that.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# I haven't tried clicking on blue cells in the bottom-right grid!
# The top-left blue cell in the bottom-right grid is at rows 36-41, cols 36-41
# Let me click at the center: row 38, col 38
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 38, 'col': 38}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
